verses.text: The book opens with its purpose: the proverbs of Solomon are given 'to know wisdom and instruction,' to grasp justice, judgment, and equity. Proverbs is a school of practical godliness. Its aim is not mere cleverness but a wise and upright life.

Wisdom is offered to the simple and the young, yet even 'a wise man will hear, and will increase learning.' None outgrows the need to learn. The truly wise are always ready to grow wiser still.

'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge,' while fools despise wisdom; a child should heed a father's instruction as a graceful ornament. Reverence for God is where wisdom starts. All true knowledge is rooted in awe of the Lord.

'My son, if sinners entice thee, consent thou not,' though they promise easy gain and shared spoil. Temptation comes with flattering invitations. The first defence against evil company is a flat refusal to go along.

He warns not to walk their road, for their feet run to evil and they lay wait for their own blood; greed 'taketh away the life of the owners thereof.' Sin destroys its servants. The grasping after ill-gotten gain ruins the very ones who chase it.

Wisdom cries aloud in the streets, asking, 'How long, ye simple ones, will ye love simplicity?' and urging them to turn at her reproof. She calls publicly and freely. God's wisdom is not hidden but pleads openly with all who will hear.

Because they refused her call and spurned her counsel, Wisdom warns she will laugh at their calamity when terror comes like a storm. Mercy scorned turns to judgment. To despise the offer of grace is to invite the ruin it would have prevented.

Then they shall call, but she will not answer, for they hated knowledge and chose not the fear of the LORD; they shall 'eat of the fruit of their own way.' There is a point when sought-for help is past. The harvest of a wilful life is its own bitter reward.

The backsliding of the simple slays them, but 'whoso hearkeneth unto me shall dwell safely... from fear of evil.' Two ends are set before all. To heed wisdom is safety; to refuse her is self-destruction.
